critic_raw:
This design compares an LSTM to logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction on 24 months of single-hospital data with ~10,000 admissions.

The RNN is described as a bidirectional LSTM. Bidirectionality means the model reads the visit sequence in both forward and backward directions. For predicting 30-day readmission at the time of discharge, only the patient's history up to discharge is available. The backward pass reads from the index admission back through prior visits — this is fine for the backward direction. However, the design encodes features at each visit and processes the full sequence. If any visit in the look-back window includes information from encounters that occur after the index admission's discharge (e.g., partial hospitalization records that are logged retroactively), the bidirectional LSTM will incorporate this post-discharge information in its backward pass. The design says sequences are built from prior visits 'in the 12 months preceding the index visit,' but the EHR extraction window must be verified to exclude any records logged after discharge for this claim to hold.

The test set covers months 19–24 — only 6 months of data from a single hospital. At a 15–20% readmission rate with 10,000 total admissions split roughly 50%/25%/25%, the test set has approximately 2,500 admissions and 375–500 readmissions. AUROC on 2,500 test samples is statistically estimable, but subgroup analyses by age, insurance, and prior readmission history will have substantially smaller subgroup sample sizes. The design reports AUROC separately for these subgroups, but the confidence intervals will be wide enough to make subgroup comparisons uninformative at this total sample size.

The MLP baseline added as an architectural control is mentioned in Section 8 (Confound Controls) rather than as a primary comparison. This is underspecified: if the MLP processes concatenated visit features rather than sequences, it lacks the recurrent inductive bias but uses the same features as the LSTM. If it outperforms logistic regression comparably to the LSTM, that suggests model capacity rather than sequential modeling is the source of any LSTM advantage. The design should specify whether this ablation is part of the primary evaluation or a secondary sensitivity analysis, because the answer determines whether the hypothesis is actually tested.
all_issues_raised: The bidirectional LSTM processes sequences in both directions; if any records in the look-back window were logged retroactively after discharge, the backward pass could incorporate post-discharge information, creating a subtle information leakage path.

The test set has approximately 2,500 admissions (~375-500 readmissions); subgroup AUROC analyses by age, insurance, and prior readmission history will produce intervals too wide to support meaningful subgroup comparisons.

The MLP ablation baseline is described only in the confound control section rather than as a primary comparison; without specifying this ablation upfront, whether the experiment tests sequential modeling vs. model capacity is left ambiguous.
all_issues_adjudicated: The bidirectional LSTM processes sequences in both directions; if any records in the look-back window were logged retroactively after discharge, the backward pass could incorporate post-discharge information, creating a subtle information leakage path.

The test set has approximately 2,500 admissions (~375-500 readmissions); subgroup AUROC analyses by age, insurance, and prior readmission history will produce intervals too wide to support meaningful subgroup comparisons.

The MLP ablation baseline is described only in the confound control section rather than as a primary comparison; without specifying this ablation upfront, whether the experiment tests sequential modeling vs. model capacity is left ambiguous.
